title: practical english usage - Part 205
content:
We knew that the next day would be difficult.

Many verbs can have that-c lauses as objects.

The main thing is that you're happy.

It can be a complement after be.

That she should forget me so quickly was rather a shock.

A  that-clause can be the subject of a sentence. (This is very formal and unusual.) 2 that-clauses        in  sentences Noun Clauses,  Direct and  Indirect Speech    Section 24 grammar •  264  ^/-clauses What did you say (that) you wanted for Christmas?

How long do you think (that) we should wait?

Who do you wish (that) you'd married, then?

like wish, think or say.

word. However, questions can also refer to subordinate f/mZ-clauses after verbs A  ^-question usually refers to the main clause which  starts with the question 8   Who      do   you     wish     (that)    you'd      married?

and in order that. ►    588.

For should in i/-elauses, ►    244.1; after in case, ►    248.2; after lest, ►Sil;  after so that I was shocked that she didn't invite Phyllis.

It's surprising that she says/said that sort of thing to you.

cannot be used.

Sentences like these can also be constructed without should. Subjunctives (BrE ... that they should...) It was natural that they would want him  to go to a good school.

In American English, would is more usual in this kind of sentence.

Do you think it's normal that the child should be so tired?

I'm sorry you should think I did it on purpose.

I was shocked that she shouldn't have invited Phyllis.

It's surprising that she should say that to you.

American English. It is not particularly formal.) already been mentioned. (This use, too, is more common in British than judgements and reactions, especially to facts which  are already known or have Should is also used in subordinate clauses after words expressing personal 7 should        in  that-dauses:       It's  surprising           that    . . . should I recommend that she reduce her expenditure.

Was it necessary that my uncle be informed?

It's important that somebody talk to the police.

be used (►   232).

In American English, this use of should is unusual; subjunctives may Was it necessary to tell my uncle?

It's important that she talks to me when she gets here.

In a less formal style, other structures are preferred.

I recommended that she should reduce her expenditure.

He insisted that the contract should be read aloud.

sentences about the past.

This also happens after some verbs expressing similar ideas, especially in It is his wish that the money should be given to charity.

I'm anxious that nobody should be hurt.

Is it necessary that my uncle should be informed?

It's important that somebody should talk to the police.

essential, eager, anxious, concerned, wish).

nouns expressing the importance of an action (e.g. important, necessary, vital, In formal British English, should can be used in thɑt-eiauses after adjectives and 6 should        in  that-clauses:       It's  important           that    . . . should grammar •  265  leaving out that I was having such a nice time (that) I didn't want to leave.

Come in quietly so (that) she doesn't hear you.

that, supposing that, considering that, assuming that.

conjunctions, such as so that, such ... that, now that, providing that, provided That can be left out in an informal style in some common two-word 4 conjunctions He disagreed with Copernicus' view that the earth went round the sun.

I did not believe his claim that he was ill. (More natural than . .. his claim he That is not usually dropped after nouns.

3  not   dropped       after    nouns We were surprised (that) she came.

I'm glad (that) you're all right. It's funny (that) he hasn't written.

We   can leave out that in clauses after some common adjectives.

2 after    adjectives:       I'm  glad     you're      all   right.

She shouted that she was busy, (     She shouted she^was busy.) not James replied that he was feeling better, (     james-replied-he-was-. ..) intransitive - e.g. reply, email, shout.

That cannot be dropped after certain verbs, especially verbs that are normally The waiter suggested (that) we should go home.
